turns.title: cli: stream refactor
turns.prompt: refactor the parser to stream instead of buffering the whole file
turns.reply: Done — `parse()` is a generator yielding records line-by-line, so memory stays flat on large inputs. Tests pass.

turns.title: cli: stream refactor
turns.reply: Added `--limit N`; it short-circuits the generator after N records. Help text updated.

turns.title: The shape of a good notebook
turns.prompt: I keep starting notebooks and abandoning them. what makes one stick?
turns.thinking: Not a product rec. Two failure modes: capture friction and retrieval failure. Name them; the rest is downstream.
turns.reply: Two things kill notebooks, neither is the app. Capture friction: if writing a thought costs more than thinking it, you won't. Retrieval failure: if you can't find it when it's relevant, the notebook is a write-only grave. It sticks when capture is near-free and past-you reliably hands present-you the right page.

turns.title: The shape of a good notebook
turns.prompt: so how do you make retrieval reliable without organizing all day?
turns.thinking: Don't say 'just search'. The move is leaving retrieval cues at write-time, in future-you's vocabulary. Cue once; the sorting never happens.
turns.reply: Stop organizing, start cueing. Leave, at write time, the two or three words future-you will actually reach for — in the vocabulary of the situation where you'll need it, not where you wrote it. A note about a bug is cued with the error message you'll paste at 2am. Cue once, retrieve forever.

turns.title: The shape of a good notebook
turns.prompt: that's basically what transcrub does, huh
turns.thinking: Yes — the marks ARE cues, the chronological feed is past-you handing over context, and surfacing the thinking is the reusable part.
turns.reply: Same shape exactly. The margin marks are cues dropped at read-time; the chronological feed hands you surrounding context instead of a naked snippet; and surfacing the thinking blocks keeps the reasoning, which is usually the reusable cue, not the conclusion.

turns.title: What is a portable Dyson sphere?
turns.prompt: explain lightcell like I'm an investor who actually knows physics
turns.thinking: The honest version, not the pitch. A gyroid radiator running hotter than the sun's surface, sealed in sapphire, fed methane — turning broadband heat into a near-monochromatic glow a matched PV drinks. Don't oversell. Name the hard part up front: the spectrum is friendly, the containment is not.
turns.reply: A LightCell is a man-made star you can hold. A gyroid lattice burns hotter than the sun's surface inside a sapphire tube, and instead of broadband heat you get a tuned glow — sodium's 589 nm line, dominant — that a bandgap-matched photovoltaic drinks at high efficiency. The trick was never making it hot. It's making the light the *right color*.

turns.title: What is a portable Dyson sphere?
turns.thinking: 589 is the sodium D doublet — streetlamp yellow. A narrow bright line matched to a cell beats a blackbody smear. And at high density the line traps: absorbed and re-emitted many times before it escapes, which reshapes the glow. The 819 nm secondary is where it gets strange — that's the part worth being honest about.
turns.reply: 589 nm is the sodium D doublet — the exact yellow of an old streetlamp. A narrow, bright line matched to a cell's bandgap converts far better than a blackbody smear across the whole spectrum. And at high sodium density the line *traps*: a photon is absorbed and re-emitted many times before it escapes, which reshapes the glow. The 819 nm secondary line is where it gets genuinely interesting — and genuinely uncertain.

turns.title: What is a portable Dyson sphere?
turns.prompt: isn't that just a sodium streetlamp
turns.reply: A sodium lamp throws its light away. A LightCell traps it and hands it to a photovoltaic — it's a streetlamp that decided to become a power plant. Same atom, opposite intent. One action becomes one eval row: point it at a real load and the spectrum either pays or it doesn't.

turns.title: What is a portable Dyson sphere?
turns.thinking: Don't launder the hard part into a footnote. The catch is real: containment at those temperatures, the trapping math at high density, keeping the spectrum from washing out. Say it plainly — a clean honest answer beats a confident smear. The physics of the glow is friendly; the engineering of holding a star still is the whole game.
turns.reply: Containment. You're running hotter than the sun's surface inside a transparent shell, so the materials and the optics are the whole game. The physics of the glow is friendly — it's holding a star still that's hard. Anyone who tells you the spectrum is the easy part is laundering the hard part into a footnote.

turns.title: field: a passing fox
turns.prompt: my model picked a fox as its avatar. is that weird?
turns.speaker: a friend

turns.title: field: a passing fox
turns.reply: Not weird — traditional. The fox is the trickster of fables; a model named for stories reaching for the fox is reading its own birth certificate. 🦊
turns.speaker: assistant
